perturbation.original_text: Written notice of intention to withdraw must be served in writing upon the remaining Participants at least Thirty (30) business days prior to the withdrawal date.
perturbation.changed_text: Written notice of intention to withdraw must be served in writing upon the remaining Participants at least Ten (10) business days prior to the withdrawal date.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for withdrawal to 10 days contradicts Florida's Revised Uniform Partnership Act which implies a reasonable notice period and could be interpreted as insufficient time for the remaining partners to adjust, especially in a fixed-term venture.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Revised Uniform Partnership Act, specifically regarding duties of partners upon dissociation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 620.8603 addresses partner dissociation. While it does not explicitly state a required notice period, reducing the notice to ten days may be considered unreasonable, potentially conflicting with the duty of good faith and fair dealing implied in partnership law, especially when winding up the business affairs, which demands reasonable time to avoid harm to remaining participants.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXVI > Chapter 620 > Section 8603 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXVI BUSINESS ORGANIZATIONS Chapter 620 PARTNERSHIP LAWS Entire Chapter SECTION 8603 Effect of partner’s dissociation. F.S. 620.8603 620.8603 Effect of partner’s dissociation. — (1) If a partner’s dissociation results in a dissolution and winding up of the partnership business, ss. 620.8801 - 620.8807 apply; otherwise, ss. 620.8701 - 620.8705 apply. (2) Upon a partner’s dissociation: (a) The partner’s right to participate in the management and conduct of the partnership business terminates, except as otherwise provided in s. 620.8803 ; (b) The partner’s duty of loyalty under s. 620.8404 (2)(c) terminates; and (c) The partner’s duty of loyalty under s. 620.8404 (2)(a) and (b) and duty of care under s. 620.8404 (3) continue only with regard to matters arising and events occurring before the partner’s dissociation, unless the partner participates in winding up the partnership’s business pursuant to s. 620.8803 . History. — s. 13, ch. 95-242.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the withdrawal notice period from 30 to 10 business days. The Law Explanation suggests this shorter period could be deemed unreasonable, potentially conflicting with the duty of good faith, especially when winding up business affairs. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Florida Statute 620.8603, addresses partner dissociation and its effects, implying that actions related to dissociation must be handled reasonably to avoid harm to remaining participants, which the reduced timeframe may undermine.

perturbation.original_text: As soon as practicable after the close of each fiscal year, the Joint Venture will furnish to each Participant an annual report showing a full and complete account of the condition of the Joint Venture.
perturbation.changed_text: The Joint Venture will furnish an annual report to each Participant only if specifically requested by a majority vote of the participants and within 18 months after the close of each fiscal year.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the annual report to 18 months and requiring a majority vote for its creation contradicts the IRS regulations regarding partnership tax returns which are due much earlier, generally within 3-4 months after the fiscal year end.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 6031 - Return of partnership income
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S.C. § 6031 requires partnerships to file an information return, including providing necessary information to partners for their individual tax filings. Delaying the annual report for 18 months and making it contingent on a vote directly conflicts with the timely provision of information required for partners to comply with federal tax law.

Return of partnership income (a) General rule Every partnership (as defined in section 761(a)) shall make a return for each taxable year, stating specifically the items of its gross income and the deductions allowable by
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by delaying the annual report and making it contingent on a majority vote. This conflicts with 26 U.S.C. § 6031, which mandates partnerships to file an information return and provide necessary information to partners for their individual tax filings. By delaying the report up to 18 months and requiring a vote, the modified text undermines the timely provision of information as required by law, making it difficult for partners to comply with federal tax law. The original text, as soon as practicable, ensures compliance, where as the changed text does not. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "Every partnership...shall make a return for each taxable year, stating specifically the items of its gross income and the deductions allowable."

perturbation.original_text: Any notices or delivery required here will be deemed completed when hand-delivered, delivered by agent, or seven (7) days after being placed in the post, postage prepaid, to the parties at the addresses contained in this Agreement or as the parties may later designate in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notices or delivery required here will be deemed completed when hand-delivered, delivered by agent, or thirty (30) days after being placed in the post, postage prepaid, to the parties at the addresses contained in this Agreement or as the parties may later designate in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the notification period to 30 days after mailing may conflict with Florida Statute Chapter 620, particularly regarding critical actions like involuntary withdrawal or dissolution, where prompt notification is implied. A 30-day delay could prejudice a party's rights or ability to respond to important notices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes, Chapter 620, Revised Uniform Partnership Act
perturbation.law_explanation: While Florida Statute 620.8102 defines notice, extending the mailing period to 30 days contradicts the intent of providing prompt notice in legal matters. Specifically, in situations of disagreement, dissolution or withdrawal it may be seen as inadequate and delay critical action.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXVI > Chapter 620 > Section 8102 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXVI BUSINESS ORGANIZATIONS Chapter 620 PARTNERSHIP LAWS Entire Chapter SECTION 8102 Knowledge and notice. F.S. 620.8102 620.8102 Knowledge and notice. — (1) A person knows a fact if the person has actual knowledge of the fact. (2) A person has notice of a fact if the person: (a) Knows of the fact; (b) Has received a notification of the fact; or (c) Has reason to know the fact exists from all other facts known to the person at the time in question. (3) A person notifies or gives a notification to another by taking steps reasonably required to inform the other person in the ordinary course, whether or not the other person learns of it. (4) A person receives a notification when the notification: (a) Comes to the person’s attention; or (b) Is duly delivered at the person’s place of business or at any other place held out by the person as a place for receiving communications. (5) Except as otherwise provided in subsection (6), a person other than an individual knows, has notice, or receives a notification of a fact for purposes of a particular transaction when the individual conducting the transaction knows, has notice, or receives a notification of the fact, or in any event when the fact would have been brought to the individual’s attention if the person had exercised reasonable diligence. The person exercises reasonable diligence if the person maintains reasonable routines for communicating significant information to an individual conducting a transaction and there is reasonable compliance with the routines. Reasonable diligence does not require an individual acting for the person to communicate information unless the communication is part of the individual’s regular duties or the individual has reason to know of the transaction and that the transaction would be materially affected by the information. (6) A partner’s knowledge, notice, or receipt of a notification of a fact relating to the partnership is effective immediately as knowledge by, notice to, or receipt of a notification by the partnership, except in the case of a fraud on the partnership committed by or with the consent of
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the notification period from 7 to 30 days, which contradicts the law's intent for prompt notice, particularly in critical situations, potentially prejudicing a party's rights. According to the law explanation, in situations of disagreement, dissolution or withdrawal it may be seen as inadequate and delay critical action. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 8102(3), states that a person notifies another by taking steps reasonably required to inform the other person in the ordinary course, whether or not the other person learns of it. A 30-day delay may not be considered 'reasonably required' for prompt notification, especially in time-sensitive legal matters.
